javascript


```
let user={ name: "Alice",age: 30,city: "Seattle" }

let values=Object.values(user)console.log(values)// ["Alice",30,"Seattle"]

```

Combine with array methods:
javascript


```
let prices={ apple: 1.5,banana: 0.75,orange: 2.0 }
let total=Object.values(prices).reduce((a,b)=> a+b,0)console.log(total)// 4.25

```

Check if a property exists:
javascript


```
let user={ name: "Alice",age: 30 }

// Using 'in' operator
console.log("name" in user)// true
console.log("email" in user)// false

// Using hasOwnProperty
console.log(user.hasOwnProperty("name"))// true
console.log(user.hasOwnProperty("email"))// false

```

 `in`  checks inherited properties too.  `hasOwnProperty`  checks only the object's own properties.

Provide defaults for missing properties:
javascript


```
let user={ name: "Alice" }

let { name,age=25 }=user
console.log(name)// "Alice"
console.log(age)// 25(default used)// In function parameters
function greet({ name,greeting="Hello" }){
  console.log(`${greeting},${name}`)}

greet({ name: "Bob" })// "Hello,Bob"

```

Objects are stored by reference,not value:
javascript


```
let obj1={ name: "Alice" }
let obj2=obj1  // Both point to same object

obj2.name="Bob"
console.log(obj1.name)// "Bob"(changed!)// Comparison checks reference
let a={ x: 1 }
let b={ x: 1 }
console.log(a===b)// false(different objects)
```

Assignment copies the reference,not the object. Changes through one variable affect all references.

Use  `?.`  to safely access nested properties:
javascript


```
let user={ name: "Alice" }

console.log(user.address?.city)// undefined(no error)console.log(user.address?.city ?? "Unknown")// "Unknown"

let user2={ address: { city: "Seattle" } }
console.log(user2.address?.city)// "Seattle"

```

Without  `?.`,accessing  `user.address.city`  would throw an error because  `address`  is undefined.

1. Confusing object references with copies
2. Forgetting that spread creates shallow copies only
3. Using  `for...in`  on arrays instead of  `for...of` 
4. Checking for property existence with  `obj.prop`(fails for falsy values)5. Mutating objects when immutability is expected
Use  `in`  or  `hasOwnProperty()`  to check existence. Use spread for immutable updates.

You now understand objects: how to create them,access properties,iterate,copy,and destructure. Objects and arrays together form the foundation of data structures in JavaScript.
In the next section,you'll combine arrays and objects to work with complex data like lists of users,product catalogs,and API responses.

You learned to create objects with literals and access properties with dot and bracket notation. You used  `Object.keys()`,`Object.values()`,and  `Object.entries()`  to iterate.
You merged objects with spread and  `Object.assign()`,and extracted values with destructuring. You understood that objects are references and spread creates shallow copies.
Remember: use  `?.`  for safe nested access,`in`  to check property existence,and spread for immutable updates.

You've learned arrays and objects separately. Now I'll show you how they combine to model real-world data.
Almost every application uses nested structures. A shopping cart is an array of product objects. A user profile contains arrays of friends and posts. APIs return objects with arrays inside. Once you can combine these,you can handle any data format.

Optional chaining(`?.`)stops evaluation if a property is null or undefined. It returns undefined instead of throwing an error.
javascript


```
const user={ profile: null };
console.log(user.profile?.name); // undefined
// Without ?.: TypeError!

```

Use  `?.`  when you're unsure if intermediate properties exist. It's safer than assuming.

You can chain multiple  `?.`  operators for deeply nested access. Each one guards against null or undefined at that level.
javascript


```
const data={};
const name=data?.user?.profile?.name;
console.log(name); // undefined(no error)
```

Without optional chaining,accessing  `data.user.profile.name`  would throw an error.

Optional chaining works with method calls too. Use  `?.()`  to safely call a method that might not exist.
javascript


```
const obj={};
obj.doSomething?.(); // undefined,no error

const arr=null;
arr?.push?.(1); // undefined,no error

```

The method only executes if it exists. Otherwise you get undefined.

To update one object in an array immutably,use  `map()`  to create a new array with the changed item.
javascript


```
const users=[{ id: 1,name: "Alice" }];
const updated=users.map(u=>
  u.id===1 ? { ...u,name: "Alicia" } : u);

```

Only the matching object is replaced. Others pass through unchanged.

You've learned to combine arrays and objects into complex data structures. You can access nested data with chaining and optional chaining(`?.`). You know how to find,filter,map,and reduce object arrays.
You've learned immutable update patterns using spread operators. You can destructure nested structures and provide defaults. These skills prepare you for working with real API data and state management in frameworks.

Programs fail. Users enter invalid data,networks drop,files go missing. Without error handling,your program crashes and users lose their work.
In this section,you'll learn to anticipate failures and respond gracefully. You'll catch errors before they crash your app,provide helpful messages,and keep your program running when things go wrong.

JavaScript has 3 types of errors. Syntax errors happen when you write invalid code. The engine catches these before running.
Runtime errors occur during execution. Accessing undefined properties or calling non-functions triggers these. Logic errors are the trickiest. Your code runs but produces wrong results. These require careful debugging.

When an error occurs,JavaScript creates an Error object. It has two main properties:  `name`(the error type)and  `message`(what went wrong).
javascript


```
const err=new Error("Something failed");
console.log(err.name);    // "Error"
console.log(err.message); // "Something failed"

```

You can create your own errors to signal problems in your code.

JavaScript has several built-in error types:

 `TypeError` : Wrong type(calling non-function)`ReferenceError` : Undefined variable
 `SyntaxError` : Invalid code structure
 `RangeError` : Number out of valid range

javascript


```
undefinedVar;        // ReferenceError
null.method();       // TypeError

```

Each type tells you what category of problem occurred.

Good error handling follows patterns:

Catch errors at boundaries(API handlers,event listeners)Don't catch errors you can't handle
Provide context in error messages
Log errors for debugging
Give users actionable feedback

Avoid catching all errors silently. Hidden failures are worse than crashes.

Stack traces show the call path that led to an error. Read from top(where error occurred)to bottom(where execution started).
text


```
TypeError: Cannot read property 'x' of undefined
    at processItem(app.js:45)at Array.forEach(<anonymous>)at processAll(app.js:30)at main(app.js:10)
```

The first line after the error message is usually where to look.

You've learned to handle errors gracefully with try/catch/finally. You can throw your own errors to signal problems and create custom error classes for your domain.
You know defensive patterns like optional chaining and nullish coalescing. You can write helpful error messages and isolate failures with error boundaries. Next,you'll learn asynchronous JavaScript,where unhandled errors can crash your entire application.

JavaScript runs on a single thread. If you wait for a slow operation(network request,file read),everything freezes. Users hate frozen interfaces.
Asynchronous code lets slow operations run in the background. Your program continues while waiting. When the operation finishes,a callback handles the result. This keeps your app responsive.

The event loop manages async operations. When you start an async task,JavaScript registers a callback and continues. When the task completes,the callback joins a queue.
The event loop checks: is the call stack empty? If yes,it takes callbacks from the queue and runs them. This cycle repeats forever,handling async results as they arrive.

Each  `.then()`  returns a new promise. If you return a value,the next  `.then()`  receives it. If you return a promise,the chain waits for it.
javascript


```
getUser(1).then(user=> getOrders(user.id)).then(orders=> getDetails(orders[0].id)).then(details=> console.log(details));

```

This flattens callback hell into a readable chain.

You've learned to write non-blocking code with callbacks,promises,and async/await. You understand the event loop and how JavaScript handles concurrent operations.
You can chain promises,handle errors,and run operations in parallel with  `Promise.all` . You know how to use  `fetch`  for HTTP requests. These async patterns are foundational for building responsive applications.

As projects grow,putting everything in one file becomes unmanageable. Modules let you split code into separate files,each with a clear purpose.
Modules provide encapsulation. Each file has its own scope. You explicitly export what others can use and import what you need. This makes code easier to understand,test,and maintain.

Before ES modules,you loaded scripts with  `<script>`  tags. All variables were global,causing naming conflicts.
html


```
<script src="utils.js"></script>
<script src="app.js"></script>

```

Order mattered. Dependencies were implicit. Large projects became tangled webs of scripts. Modules solved these problems.

Import specific exports using curly braces. Names must match what was exported.
javascript


```
import { PI,double } from "./utils.js";

console.log(PI);        // 3.14159
console.log(double(5)); // 10

```

You only import the bindings you need. Bundlers can remove unused exports during build(tree-shaking).

Rename imports with the  `as`  keyword when names conflict or you prefer different names locally.
javascript


```
import { add as sum } from "./math.js";
import { add as concat } from "./string.js";

console.log(sum(1,2));       // 3
console.log(concat("a","b")); // "ab"

```

The exported name stays the same; only your local name changes.

You can also rename when exporting. This lets you use one name internally and expose a different name.
javascript


```
function internalHelper(){}

export { internalHelper as helper };

```

Or rename multiple exports at once:
javascript


```
export { func1 as a,func2 as b };

```

Import default exports without curly braces. You can name them whatever you want.
javascript


```
import User from "./User.js";
import MyUser from "./User.js"; // Same thing,different name

const user=new User("Alice");

```

The name you choose doesn't need to match anything in the exporting module.

Key differences:

ESM:  `import` / `export`,static analysis,async loading
CJS:  `require` / `module.exports`,dynamic,sync loading

ESM supports tree-shaking(removing unused code). CJS runs  `require`  at runtime. Modern JavaScript prefers ESM,but CJS remains common in Node.js.

Follow these practices for clean module structure:

One concept per module
Keep modules focused and small
Use index files for public APIs
Prefer named exports for utilities
Use default exports for main classes
Avoid side effects in module top-level code

Common patterns for organizing modules:

Feature folders: group by feature(user/,products/)Layer folders: group by role(components/,services/)Index exports: expose public API via index.js

text


```
src/
  user/
    UserService.js
    UserModel.js
    index.js

```

Bundlers like Webpack,Rollup,and esbuild combine modules into optimized bundles for production.
They resolve imports,bundle code into fewer files,minify output,and enable tree-shaking. Most production apps use a bundler,though modern browsers can handle unbundled ES modules.

You've learned to organize JavaScript into modules using ES module syntax. You can use named and default exports,import what you need,and re-export to create clean APIs.
You understand dynamic imports for code splitting and the difference between ES modules and CommonJS. These skills help you build maintainable,scalable applications.

Object-oriented programming(OOP)organizes code around objects that combine data and behavior. Instead of scattered functions and variables,you group related things together.
OOP helps model real-world entities. A User object has properties(name,email)and methods(login,logout). This makes code intuitive and maintainable.

Use  `instanceof`  to check if an object is an instance of a class.
javascript


```
class Animal {}
class Dog extends Animal {}

const dog=new Dog();

console.log(dog instanceof Dog);    // true
console.log(dog instanceof Animal); // true
console.log(dog instanceof Object); // true

```

 `instanceof`  checks the entire prototype chain.

Common patterns in OOP JavaScript:

Singleton: One instance only
Observer: Notify listeners of changes
Strategy: Swap algorithms at runtime
Decorator: Add behavior to objects

These patterns appear in frameworks and libraries. Recognizing them helps you understand codebases faster.

You've learned to create classes with constructors,methods,and fields. You can use private fields for encapsulation and static members for class-level functionality.
You understand inheritance with extends and super,plus alternatives like composition and mixins. These OOP concepts help you write organized,reusable code that models real-world entities effectively.

The Document Object Model(DOM)is a tree representation of your HTML page. JavaScript can read and modify this tree,changing what users see without reloading the page.
Every HTML element becomes a node in the tree. You can find nodes,change their content,add new ones,or remove them entirely. This is how modern web apps create dynamic interfaces.

The  `document`  object is your entry point to the DOM. It represents the entire HTML document and provides methods to find and create elements.
javascript


```
console.log(document.title); // Page title
console.log(document.body);  // <body> element
console.log(document.head);  // <head> element

```

All DOM manipulation starts with  `document` .

The DOM is a tree. Each element has a parent(except the root),and can have children and siblings. html ```

First

Second
``` Understanding these relationships helps you traverse and manipulate the DOM efficiently.

Use  `getElementById()`  to find an element by its ID attribute. IDs should be unique on the page.
javascript


```
const header=document.getElementById("header");
console.log(header.textContent);

```

This returns the element directly,or  `null`  if not found. It's the fastest way to select a single element.

`getElementsByClassName()`  returns all elements with a given class. It returns a live HTMLCollection.
javascript


```
const items=document.getElementsByClassName("item");
console.log(items.length);
console.log(items[0]); // First element

```

"Live" means the collection updates automatically when the DOM changes.

`getElementsByTagName()`  returns all elements of a given type. Like class selection,it returns a live collection.
javascript


```
const paragraphs=document.getElementsByTagName("p");
const divs=document.getElementsByTagName("div");

```

Use this when you need all elements of a specific type.

`querySelectorAll()`  returns all elements matching a CSS selector. Unlike  `getElementsByClassName`,it returns a static NodeList.
javascript


```
const items=document.querySelectorAll(".item");
items.forEach(item=> console.log(item));

```

"Static" means the list doesn't update when the DOM changes.

Read element content with `textContent`(just text)or `innerHTML`(HTML markup). javascript ``` const el=document.querySelector("p"); console.log(el.textContent); // "Hello" console.log(el.innerHTML); // "Hello" ``` `textContent` is safer and faster. Use `innerHTML` only when you need HTML parsing.

Insert elements at specific positions with these methods:
javascript


```
parent.prepend(el);        // First child
parent.append(el);         // Last child
el.before(newEl);          // Before el
el.after(newEl);           // After el
parent.insertBefore(newEl,referenceEl);

```

Choose the method that matches where you need the element.

Use  `cloneNode()`  to copy an element. Pass  `true`  for a deep clone that includes children.
javascript


```
const original=document.querySelector(".template");
const shallow=original.cloneNode();     // Just the element
const deep=original.cloneNode(true);    // With children

```

Clones don't copy event listeners unless you add them manually.

Move between elements using relationship properties:
javascript


```
const el=document.querySelector("li");
el.parentElement;        // Parent
el.children;             // Child elements
el.firstElementChild;    // First child element
el.nextElementSibling;   // Next sibling element
el.previousElementSibling;

```

Use  `Element`  versions to skip text nodes.
The `

Data attributes(`data-*`)store custom data on HTML elements. Access them through the `dataset` property. html ```
``` javascript ``` const el=document.querySelector("div"); console.log(el.dataset.userId); // "123" console.log(el.dataset.role); // "admin" el.dataset.status="active"; ``` Hyphenated names become camelCase in JavaScript.

DOM operations are slow compared to JavaScript. Optimize by:

Minimizing DOM queries(cache references)Batching updates with fragments
Using  `textContent`  over  `innerHTML` 
Avoiding layout thrashing(read,then write)javascript


```
// Bad: query each time
for(...)document.querySelector(".x").style...

// Good: cache reference
const el=document.querySelector(".x");
for(...)el.style...

```

You've learned to select elements with  `getElementById`,`querySelector`,and related methods. You can read and modify content,attributes,classes,and styles.
You know how to create,insert,and remove elements,plus traverse the DOM tree. You understand performance best practices like caching references and using document fragments. Next,you'll learn to respond to user actions with events.

Events are the browser's way of telling your code something happened. A user clicked a button. A form was submitted. A key was pressed. The page finished loading.
You write event handlers that run when specific events occur. This makes your pages interactive,responding to user actions in real time.

Common event types include:

Mouse:  `click`,`dblclick`,`mouseenter`,`mouseleave` 
Keyboard:  `keydown`,`keyup` 
Form:  `submit`,`change`,`input`,`focus`,`blur` 
Window:  `load`,`resize`,`scroll` 

Each element type supports different events relevant to its purpose.

You've learned to respond to user actions with event listeners. You understand the event object,preventing defaults,and how events bubble through the DOM.
You know event delegation for efficient handling and patterns like debouncing and throttling. You can create custom events for component communication. Next,you'll explore browser APIs that extend what JavaScript can do.

Browsers provide APIs that extend JavaScript's capabilities. Store data locally,track location,copy to clipboard,send notifications,and more.
These APIs let you build features that would be impossible with JavaScript alone. Each API has its own methods and often requires user permission.

`sessionStorage`  works like localStorage but clears when the tab closes.
javascript


```
sessionStorage.setItem("temp","value");
const temp=sessionStorage.getItem("temp");

```

Use sessionStorage for temporary data like form progress or wizard state. Use localStorage for persistent preferences.

The History API lets you control browser history and move through it programmatically.
javascript


```
history.back();    // Go back
history.forward(); // Go forward
history.go(-2);    // Go back 2 pages

// Add history entry without page load
history.pushState({ page: 2 },"","/page2");

```

Single-page apps use  `pushState`  to update the URL without reloading.

The  `location`  object provides information about the current URL and methods to change it.
javascript


```
console.log(location.href);     // Full URL
console.log(location.pathname); // "/products"
console.log(location.search);   // "?id=123"
console.log(location.hash);     // "#section"

location.href="https://example.com"; // Go to URL

```

You've explored the browser APIs that extend JavaScript's capabilities. You can store data with localStorage and sessionStorage,manage navigation with History API,and access the clipboard and geolocation.
You know Intersection Observer for visibility detection and Web Workers for background processing. These APIs let you build sophisticated web applications with native features.

TypeScript is JavaScript with static type checking. You declare what types variables and functions expect,and TypeScript catches errors before you run the code.
typescript


```
let name: string="Alice";
name=42; // Error: Type 'number' is not assignable to 'string'

```

TypeScript compiles to plain JavaScript. Browsers and Node run the compiled output.

TypeScript catches bugs early. Without types,errors hide until runtime. With types,your editor shows them as you write.
Benefits include:

Catch typos and type errors immediately
Better autocomplete and documentation
Safer refactoring
Self-documenting code

Most large JavaScript projects now use TypeScript.

TypeScript infers types from assignments. You don't need to annotate everything.
typescript


```
let count=10;        // inferred: number
let message="hello"; // inferred: string
const items=[1,2];  // inferred: number[]

count="ten"; // Error! TypeScript knows count is a number

```

Annotate when inference isn't clear or for function parameters.

Literal types restrict values to specific strings or numbers:
typescript


```
type Direction="north" | "south" | "east" | "west";
type DiceRoll=1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6;

let dir: Direction="north"; // OK
dir="up"; // Error: not one of the allowed values

```

This catches typos and invalid values at compile time.

Tuples are arrays with fixed length and types at each position:
typescript


```
let point: [number,number]=[10,20];
let entry: [string,number]=["age",25];

const [x,y]=point; // x and y are numbers

point=[1,2,3]; // Error: too many elements

```

Tuples are useful for returning multiple values from functions.

`any`  disables type checking. Use sparingly as a last resort.
typescript


```
let value: any="hello";
value=42;           // No error
value.foo.bar.baz();  // No error(but crashes at runtime!)
```

 `any`  defeats the purpose of TypeScript. Prefer  `unknown`  when you don't know the type.

Set up TypeScript in your project:
bash


```
npm install typescript --save-dev
npx tsc --init  # Creates tsconfig.json

```

The  `tsconfig.json`  file configures compilation options. Start with  `strict: true`  for maximum type safety. Run  `npx tsc`  to compile  `.ts`  files to  `.js` .

Most frameworks support TypeScript:

React: Use  `.tsx`  files,types for props
Vue: Built-in support with  `<script lang="ts">` 
Node: Use  `ts-node`  or compile before running
Express: Install  `@types/express`  for types

The  `@types/*`  packages provide types for JavaScript libraries.

You've learned TypeScript fundamentals. You can add type annotations to variables,functions,and objects. You understand union types,literal types,and generics.
You know the difference between  `any`  and  `unknown`,and when to use type assertions. TypeScript catches bugs at compile time and makes your code more maintainable. This completes your JavaScript and TypeScript foundation.

Tests verify your code works correctly. Without tests,you discover bugs when users complain. With tests,you catch them before deployment.
Tests also give you confidence to refactor. When tests pass after changes,you know you didn't break anything. This makes maintenance less stressful and more productive.

Tests come in 3 main types:

Unit tests: Test individual functions in isolation
Integration tests: Test how parts work together
End-to-end tests: Test the full application flow

Unit tests are fast and numerous. E2E tests are slow but thorough. A good test suite has many unit tests,some integration tests,and few E2E tests.

Test frameworks provide structure for writing and running tests. Popular choices:

Jest: Full-featured,works out of the box
Vitest: Fast,Vite-native
Mocha: Flexible,needs assertion library

We'll use Jest syntax,but concepts apply to all frameworks.

Matchers define how to compare values:
javascript


```
expect(value).toBe(5);           // Strict equality
expect(value).toEqual({a: 1});   // Deep equality
expect(value).toBeTruthy();      // Truthy check
expect(value).toBeNull();        // Is null
expect(value).toBeDefined();     // Not undefined
expect(value).toContain('x');    // Array/string contains

```

`toBe`  uses  `Object.is()`(similar to  `===`  but treats  `NaN`  as equal to  `NaN`).  `toEqual`  compares values deeply.
javascript


```
const obj={ a: 1 };

expect(obj).toBe({ a: 1 });    // FAILS(different objects)expect(obj).toEqual({ a: 1 }); // PASSES(same values)expect(5).toBe(5);             // PASSES(primitives)expect([1,2]).toEqual([1,2]); // PASSES(arrays)
```

Use  `toBe`  for primitives,`toEqual`  for objects and arrays.

Code coverage shows which lines your tests execute:
bash


```
jest --coverage

```

Coverage reports show:

Lines: Percentage of lines executed
Branches: Percentage of if/else branches tested
Functions: Percentage of functions called

Aim for high coverage,but don't chase 100%. Focus on testing logic,not every line.

TDD follows a cycle:
1. Write a failing test for new functionality
2. Write minimal code to make it pass
3. Refactor while keeping tests green
TDD forces you to think about requirements before coding. Tests become documentation for expected behavior.

Test these things:

Public functions and their return values
Edge cases(empty inputs,boundaries)Error conditions
State changes

Don't test:

Third-party library internals
Trivial code(getters,setters)Implementation details that may change

Common ways to run tests:
bash


```
npm test              # Run all tests
npm test -- --watch   # Watch mode(re-run on changes)npm test -- myfile    # Run tests matching 'myfile'
npm test -- -t 'add'  # Run tests with 'add' in name

```

Watch mode is great during development. It re-runs relevant tests as you save files.

You've learned to write tests with Jest and similar frameworks. You can use matchers to verify values,organize tests with describe blocks,and handle async code.
You understand mocking for isolating code and testing DOM interactions. You know what to test and how to write testable code. Testing gives you confidence that your code works and stays working.

Debugging is finding and fixing errors in code. Bugs are inevitable. What matters is how quickly you can identify and resolve them.
Good debugging combines tools,techniques,and systematic thinking. You'll learn to use browser dev tools,read error messages,and methodically isolate problems.

The console offers more than  `log()` :
javascript


```
console.log('Basic output');
console.warn('Warning message');  // Yellow
console.error('Error message');   // Red
console.table([{a:1},{a:2}]);    // Table format
console.group('Group');           // Collapsible group
console.groupEnd();
console.time('timer'); /* code */ console.timeEnd('timer');

```

Error messages contain valuable information:
text


```
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined(reading 'x')at processUser(app.js:25:10)at main(app.js:10:3)
```

This tells you:

Error type: TypeError
What happened: Accessing 'x' on undefined
Where: app.js line 25,column 10
Call path: main called processUser

Know these common errors:

 `TypeError` : Wrong type(null.method())`ReferenceError` : Undefined variable
 `SyntaxError` : Invalid code structure
 `RangeError` : Value out of range
 `URIError` : Malformed URI(bad encodeURI input)The error type guides you toward the problem category.

Open DevTools with F12 or Cmd/Ctrl+Shift+I. Main panels:

Console: Run code,see logs and errors
Elements: Inspect and modify DOM/CSS
Sources: Debug JavaScript with breakpoints
Network: Monitor HTTP requests
Application: View storage,cookies,cache

Learn keyboard shortcuts to work efficiently.

Breakpoints pause execution at specific lines. In the Sources panel,click a line number to set one.
When paused,you can:

Inspect variable values
Step through code line by line
See the call stack
Evaluate expressions in console

Breakpoints are more thorough than console.log for complex bugs.

Add watch expressions in DevTools to monitor values:

In Sources panel,find the Watch section
Click+to add expressions like  `user.name`  or  `items.length` 
Values update as you step through code

Watch expressions help track how data changes during execution.

The call stack shows the chain of function calls that led to the current point:
text


```
processItem    <- You are here
processAll
handleClick(anonymous)
```

Click entries to jump to that code. This helps understand the path through your program.

The Network panel shows all HTTP activity:

Red entries indicate failed requests
Click a request to see headers,payload,response
Filter by type(XHR,JS,CSS,etc.)Check timing to find slow requests

Use this to debug API calls,authentication issues,and loading problems.

Async bugs are tricky because code doesn't run linearly. Tips:

Add breakpoints inside  `.then()`  and  `async`  functions
Use  `console.trace()`  to see where async code was triggered
Look at the call stack when paused to see the full async chain(modern DevTools show this by default)Watch for unhandled promise rejections in console

The Performance panel records and analyzes execution:
1. Click Record
2. Perform the slow action
3. Stop recording
4. Analyze the flame chart
Long bars indicate slow functions. Use this to find performance bottlenecks.

The Memory panel helps find leaks:

Take heap snapshots to see memory usage
Compare snapshots to find growing objects
Look for detached DOM nodes
Check for forgotten event listeners or timers

Memory leaks cause your app to slow down over time.

Follow a systematic approach:
1. Reproduce the bug consistently
2. Isolate the problem area
3. Form a hypothesis
4. Test your hypothesis
5. Fix and verify
Don't randomly change code hoping it works. Understand the bug first.

When you can't find a bug,use binary search:
1. Comment out half your code
2. If bug disappears,it's in the commented half
3. Uncomment and comment the other half of that section
4. Repeat until you find the exact line
This finds bugs in logarithmic time,much faster than checking line by line.

Explain your code to someone(or something,like a rubber duck). The act of explaining often reveals the bug.
As you describe what each line does,you might say "and here it should..." and realize it doesn't. Many bugs are found by simply talking through the code.

Source maps connect minified/bundled code back to original source:
javascript


```
// In bundler config
devtool: 'source-map'

```

With source maps,DevTools shows your original files even when the browser runs bundled code. Breakpoints work on your source,not the minified version.

You've learned to use browser DevTools effectively. You can set breakpoints,step through code,and inspect variables. You understand how to read error messages and the call stack.
You know debugging strategies like binary search and rubber duck debugging. These skills help you find and fix bugs efficiently,saving hours of frustration.

A well-organized project is easier to understand,maintain,and scale. Poor organization leads to confusion,bugs,and slow development.
You'll learn common patterns for structuring JavaScript projects. These conventions help teams work together and make onboarding new developers faster.

Most JavaScript projects follow this structure:
text


```
project/
  src/           # Source code
  tests/         # Test files
  dist/          # Built output
  node_modules/  # Dependencies
  package.json   # Project config

```

The  `src`  folder contains your code. Keep it separate from configuration,tests,and build output.

Feature-based organization groups all files for a feature together:
text


```
src/
  users/
    UserList.js
    UserService.js
    userUtils.js
    users.test.js
  products/
    ProductCard.js
    ProductService.js

```

This lets you quickly find all code related to a feature.

Type-based organization groups files by their role:
text


```
src/
  components/
    UserList.js
    ProductCard.js
  services/
    UserService.js
    ProductService.js
  utils/
    format.js

```

This works well for smaller projects. Feature-based scales better for large codebases.

`dependencies`  are needed to run your app.  `devDependencies`  are only for development.
bash


```
npm install express        # Goes to dependencies
npm install jest --save-dev # Goes to devDependencies

```

Test frameworks,linters,and build tools are devDependencies. Libraries your code imports are dependencies.

Define scripts in package.json for common tasks:
json


```
"scripts": {
  "start": "node src/index.js","dev": "nodemon src/index.js","build": "webpack","test": "jest","lint": "eslint src/"
}

```

Run them with  `npm run <script>` .  `start`  and  `test`  don't need  `run` .

ESLint finds problems and enforces code style:
bash


```
npm install eslint --save-dev
npm init @eslint/config@latest

```

ESLint catches:

Unused variables
Missing semicolons(if configured)Potential bugs
Style inconsistencies

Run it in your editor for real-time feedback.

Prettier automatically formats code:
bash


```
npm install prettier --save-dev

```

Create a  `.prettierrc`  file:
json


```
{
  "semi": true,"singleQuote": true,"tabWidth": 2
}

```

Prettier handles formatting. ESLint handles code quality. Use both together.

The  `.gitignore`  file excludes files from version control:
text


```
node_modules/
dist/
.env
*.log
.DS_Store
coverage/

```

Never commit  `node_modules`(too large)or  `.env`(contains secrets). Commit  `package-lock.json`  to ensure consistent installs.

Store configuration in environment variables,not code:
text


```
# .env file
API_KEY=secret123
DATABASE_URL=postgres://...

```

javascript


```
const apiKey=process.env.API_KEY;

```

Use  `dotenv`  package to load  `.env`  files in development. Never commit  `.env`  files.

Projects often have multiple config files:

 `package.json`  - Project metadata
 `eslint.config.js`  - Linting rules
 `.prettierrc`  - Formatting rules
 `tsconfig.json`  - TypeScript config
 `.env`  - Environment variables
 `jest.config.js`  - Test config

Each tool looks for its own config file.

Build tools prepare code for production:

Webpack: Bundle modules,handle assets
Vite: Fast dev server,modern bundling
esbuild: Fast JavaScript bundler
Babel: Transform modern JS to older syntax

Build tools combine files,minify code,and optimize for browsers.

Clean code is readable and maintainable:

Use descriptive names for variables and functions
Keep functions short and focused
Avoid deep nesting
Don't repeat yourself(DRY)Write code for humans,not just computers

Code is read more often than written. Optimize for readability.

Git best practices:

Commit often with clear messages
Use branches for features
Keep commits focused on one change
Pull before pushing to avoid conflicts
Write descriptive commit messages

Good commit history helps debugging and understanding project evolution.

You've learned to structure JavaScript projects for maintainability. You understand package.json,npm scripts,and the difference between dependencies and devDependencies.
You know how to use linting and formatting tools,manage environment variables,and write clean,readable code. These practices make projects easier to work on alone or in teams.

You've built a solid JavaScript foundation. You understand variables,functions,objects,arrays,async code,the DOM,and more.

PostgreSQL: Relational,SQL-based,reliable
MongoDB: Document-based,flexible schema
Redis: In-memory,fast caching

DELETE /users/1 - Delete user

Utility types(Partial,Pick,Omit)Conditional types
Template literal types
Declaration files

Expand your testing knowledge:

Integration tests: Test components working together
End-to-end tests: Test full user flows(Cypress,Playwright)Visual regression: Catch UI changes
Performance testing: Measure speed

HTTPS: Encrypt all traffic

Performance matters for user experience:

Image optimization: Compress and resize

Measure before optimizing. Use Lighthouse and browser DevTools.

Accessible apps work for all users:

Color contrast: Readable for all

Accessibility isn't optional. It's a legal requirement in many places.

Write about what you learned
